description: This plot demonstrates the variation of yield stress (in MPa, marked by black triangles) and ductility (in %, marked by black circles) of as-cast or annealed polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys as a function of Al content (ranging from 0 to 40 at.%), with data collected from previous references and the present study. Yield stress first increases with rising Al content, reaches a maximum of ~650 MPa at ~25 at.% Al, then drops to ~320 MPa before a slight rebound in the 30-40 at.% Al range. Ductility generally decreases as Al content increases, from ~35% at 15 at.% Al to ~2% at 40 at.% Al, with a small ductility peak observed at ~28 at.% Al. This figure validates the composition-dependent mechanical property trends of Fe-Al alloys, provides performance benchmarks for subsequent work hardening research, and the observed property variations are associated with alloy ordering and dislocation type transition with increasing Al content.

This plot presents the variation of work hardening rate (WHR, measured between 1% and 1.5% plastic strain) of as-cast or annealed polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys as a function of Al content (0 to 40 at.%), including data from prior literature and the present study. Black circles represent binary Fe-Al alloys, while two separate data points correspond to Fe-Al alloys doped with ~0.1 at.% B. For binary Fe-Al, WHR stays below 2 GPa when Al content is lower than 20 at.%, rises sharply to a peak of ~16 GPa at 28–29 at.% Al, then decreases to ~5–6 GPa when Al content exceeds 35 at.%. Addition of 0.1 at.% B shows no consistent significant effect on WHR. This figure supports the core conclusion that ordered Fe-Al alloys exhibit higher WHR, with the sharp peak at 26–29 at.% Al attributed to hardening mechanisms related to superdislocations, superdislocation dipoles and sessile dislocation reactions in ordered phases.

This chart plots the variation of anti-phase boundary (APB) fault energies (unit: mJ/m²) as a function of aluminium content (25% to 37 at.%) in Fe-Al alloys with different ordered structures. Three data series are presented: APB I energy in B2 ordered Fe-Al alloys (solid dots with solid line) increases linearly with increasing Al content, from ~27 mJ/m² at 27 at.% Al to ~109 mJ/m² at 36 at.% Al; APB I energy in DO₃ ordered Fe-Al alloys (solid squares with short dashed line) stays within 65-80 mJ/m² in the 25 to 29 at.% Al range; APB II energy in DO₃ ordered Fe-Al alloys (hollow triangles with long dashed line) decreases linearly with increasing Al content, from ~85 mJ/m² at 26 at.% Al to ~35 mJ/m² at 30 at.% Al. The data are cited from previous literature, and are used to interpret the dislocation configurations and work hardening behavior of Fe-Al alloys: low APB energies in DO₃ ordered low-Al Fe-Al alloys lead to dissociated superdislocations, while higher APB energies in B2 ordered high-Al alloys keep superdislocations closely coupled.

This plot presents the variation of elastic stiffness constants and Zener anisotropy factor of Fe-Al alloys as a function of Al content ranging from 0 at.% to 40 at.%. The x-axis is Aluminium content (%), left y-axis represents stiffness constants in GPa, right y-axis denotes anisotropy factor. Three datasets are plotted: solid circles correspond to C44, solid squares correspond to (C11-C12)/2, open triangles correspond to the anisotropy factor A. C44 increases gradually with rising Al content, reaches a peak of ~130 GPa around 27 at.% Al, then decreases slightly. (C11-C12)/2 decreases with increasing Al content first, drops to a minimum of ~20 GPa in the 25-30 at.% Al range, then rises again. The anisotropy factor A shows a sharp peak of ~6.5 in the 25-30 at.% Al range, indicating extremely high elastic anisotropy for Fe3Al-type alloys. The figure demonstrates that the high elastic anisotropy in this composition range arises from both the increase of C44 and decrease of (C11-C12)/2, providing fundamental elastic property data for explaining the work hardening peak and dislocation configuration evolution in Fe-Al alloys during deformation.

This is a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Fe-15at.%Al alloy after room temperature compression deformation to 2-3% strain at a nominal strain rate of 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The included scale bar is 0.25μm, the zone (stress) axis is close to [0 1 3], and the arrow marks the imaging g vector of 2 0 0. The microstructure shows single dislocations distributed in a loose cellular pattern, with weak dislocation clustering forming an incipient cell structure, and many dislocation segments in clustered regions are identified as dipoles. The sample was prepared from induction-melted drop-cast ingots, machined into compression samples before deformation, with TEM foils fabricated via low-temperature electropolishing. This observation confirms that low-Al Fe-Al alloys with disordered or short-range ordered structure deform via glide of single (1/2)<111> dislocations, and the observed dislocation clustering and dipole formation are structural origins of the early-stage work hardening behavior of this alloy system.

This set of two bright-field transmission electron microscopy micrographs characterizes the dislocation structure of Fe-20at.%Al alloy after 2-3% room temperature compressive deformation, with zone (stress) axis [0 1 1] and imaging vector parallel to 011 (marked by the arrow). The two micrographs are collected using opposite sign imaging vectors, with a scale bar of 0.2μm provided. Regions marked "d" are dislocation segments and loops with dipole arrangement, validated by the relative displacement of dislocation images between the two micrographs. Curled single (1/2)<111> dislocations are the dominant deformation defects observed, matching the deformation mechanism of low-Al Fe-Al alloys with short-range order. This figure confirms that single dislocation glide and dipole formation are contributors to work hardening in the Fe-20at.%Al alloy system.

This is a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Fe-20at.%Al alloy after 2-3% room temperature compressive deformation, with a scale bar of 0.2 μm. The observation is performed under a zone axis near [0 0 1], using imaging g vector 1 1 0 marked by the arrow. The microstructure shows straight screw dislocations, some of which are loosely paired, narrow edge dipole segments, and abundant small dipole loops. The tested Fe-20at.%Al alloy has a bcc structure with short-range order, and its deformation is dominated by glide of single dislocations with Burgers vector (1/2)<1 1 1>. The observed dipole loops and segments are confirmed to be generated by trailing from gliding screw dislocations. This figure supports the conclusion that single dislocation motion and dipole formation contribute to work hardening in low-Al content Fe-Al alloys.

This figure contains two transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of deformed Fe-25at.% Al alloy with DO3 and B2 long-range order. The left image is a dark-field (DF) micrograph acquired using the 100 diffraction beam under Bragg condition, while the right image is a weak-beam dark-field (WBDF) micrograph with a 0.2 μm scale bar, acquired using the 200 beam with 400 beam excited to enable both fine dislocation contrast and residual anti-phase boundary (APB) contrast, with the arrow indicating the <100> imaging g vector. In the left DF image, 't' labels thermal APBs from coarse B2 domains, and 's' labels deformation-induced APBI faults formed by glide of 1/2<111> dislocations. Both micrographs show loosely coupled dislocation pairs connected by low-energy APBs, verifying that APB energies in this alloy are insufficiently high to form closely coupled superdislocations, which supports the interpretation of work hardening behavior in ordered Fe-Al alloys.

This is a bright-field transmission electron micrograph of room-temperature deformed Fe-25at.%Al alloy, with a scale bar of 0.2μm. The micrograph is acquired at zone axis near [0 0 1] using a <1 1 0> imaging vector marked by the arrow. Regions marked 'p' correspond to dislocation pairs, while regions marked 'd' correspond to dislocation dipoles. A large number of loosely coupled near-screw orientation dislocation pairs are observed, indicating imperfect DO3 order in the alloy and sufficiently low anti-phase boundary (APB) energy that superdislocations dissociate into loosely coupled pairs gliding with dragged APBs. This figure provides microstructural evidence for the work hardening mechanism of medium-Al Fe-Al alloys, confirming low APB energy leads to loosely coupled dislocations rather than closely bonded superdislocations.

This set of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs shows the dislocation structure of room-temperature 2~3% compressed Fe-28at.%Al alloy, which corresponds to the composition with peak work hardening rate in the Fe-Al alloy system. The scale bar is 0.5μm, the zone axis is near [0 0 1], and the imaging vector marked by the arrow is 2 0 0. Abundant superdislocations are observed, most of which are straight, in near-screw orientations, and lie on {1 1 0} glide planes with different Burgers vectors. These superdislocations are perfect superdislocations for B2 lattice composed of two 1/2<111> partials, trailing APBII faults during gliding. The uniform distribution of these near-screw superdislocations, together with subsequently formed dislocation dipoles and sessile <100> edge dislocations from superdislocation reactions, contributes to the sharp work hardening peak in 26~30at.% Al Fe-Al alloys.

This group of two bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs are collected under +g and -g imaging conditions, showing dislocation structures in room-temperature deformed Fe-28at.%Al alloy, the composition corresponding to the work hardening peak of the investigated Fe-Al alloy series. The left micrograph carries a 0.2μm scale bar. Dipole dislocation pairs are marked as 'd', while non-dipole dislocation pairs with identical Burgers vectors are marked as 'p'. The observation zone axis is near [0 0 1], and the ±<2 0 0> imaging g vector is marked by the arrow in each micrograph. The contrast reversal of dipole pairs between the two images confirms their configuration. This figure provides direct microstructural evidence that abundant dislocation dipoles and paired superdislocations form after deformation, supporting the work hardening mechanism for the alloy system.

This figure displays a pair of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs captured under +g and -g imaging conditions, with a 0.2 μm scale bar provided. The tested sample is Fe–28at.% Al alloy subjected to 2~3% room temperature compression after induction melting, drop casting, and slow cooling to obtain B2 and DO3 ordered structure. The imaging zone axis is near [0 0 1], and the applied imaging vector is of 110 type. Straight and parallel reaction dislocations are marked with "r" in the second micrograph, and the two micrographs compare dislocation characteristics under opposite diffraction vectors to verify dislocation reaction behaviors. This observation confirms sessile reaction dislocations form during plastic deformation of Fe-28% Al, supporting the conclusion that the work hardening peak in 26~30at.% Al Fe-Al alloys partially originates from these sessile dislocations acting as barriers for dislocation motion.

This is a weak-beam transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph of deformed Fe-28at.%Al alloy, with a scale bar of 0.2μm. Two insets provide magnified views of the superdislocation reaction features marked 'r' in the main image. The imaging conditions are: zone axis near [0 0 1], imaging g vector (arrowed) is 2 0 0, under near g:3–4g condition. The features marked 'r' are identified as sessile <1 0 0> edge dislocations formed via reaction of two glissile <1 1 1> superdislocations lying on different {1 1 0} glide planes. These sessile dislocation locks restrict further motion of superdislocations at room temperature, providing direct microstructural evidence for the sharp work hardening peak observed in Fe-Al alloys with 26~30at.% Al content.

This is a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph of deformed Fe-30at.% Al alloy after 2-3% room temperature compression, with a scale bar of 0.2 μm. The zone axis is near [0 0 1], and the arrow marks the imaging vector g=110. The micrograph displays abundant straight dislocation segments, alongside stepped dislocation structures formed via dislocation reactions. The studied alloy has a B2 ordered structure with weak DO3 order, and the observed dislocation pairs are products of dislocation interactions rather than partial dislocations of glissile superdislocations, as the high antiphase boundary energy leads to small separation between partial dislocations constituting superdislocations. This figure supports the conclusion that dislocation reactions and the resulting sessile dislocation structures contribute to the sharp work hardening peak observed in Fe-Al alloys with 26~30at.% Al content.

This figure contains two bright-field transmission electron microscopy micrographs capturing dislocation structures in Fe–30 at.% Al alloy after 2-3% room temperature compression. The left low-magnification micrograph (scale bar: 0.25 μm) shows overall high-density dislocations distributed across the alloy matrix. The right higher-magnification micrograph (scale bar: 0.1 μm) highlights paired dislocations marked as 'd', which are confirmed as dipole segments and dislocation loops. The observation is performed with zone axis near [1 1 1] and arrow-indicated imaging vector of 1 1 0. The morphologies verify that deformation of this high Al content Fe-Al alloy is dominated by <111> superdislocation motion: high antiphase boundary energy results in small partial dislocation separation, so the observed parallel dislocation pairs are dipoles formed by dislocation reactions rather than glissile superdislocation partials. This provides direct microstructural evidence to support the work hardening mechanism analysis for Fe-Al alloy systems.

This is a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph of room-temperature deformed Fe-30at.% Al alloy, with a scale bar of 0.2μm. The arrow in the figure indicates the 110 imaging vector, and the test zone axis is near [0 0 1]. Dislocation segments and background fringes are clearly observed: the dislocations are superdislocations of the B2 lattice, constituted by two partial dislocations with Burgers vector (1/2)<111>, which lie close to each other due to high anti-phase boundary (APB) fault energy; the background fringes are residual APBII contrast produced during deformation of the weakly DO3 ordered material. Similar dislocation structures are also confirmed in deformed Fe-35at.% Al alloy. This figure provides direct microstructural evidence for analyzing superdislocation motion, APB formation, and the work hardening mechanism of ordered Fe-Al alloys.

This figure contains two bright-field transmission electron microscopy micrographs showing typical dislocation structures of Fe-35at.%Al alloy with B2 ordered structure after 2-3% room temperature compression deformation. The left micrograph has a scale bar of 0.25 μm, and the higher magnification right micrograph has a scale bar of 0.1 μm. For both micrographs, the zone axis is near [1 1 0] and the imaging vector g (marked by the arrow) is 1 1 0 (barred). A large number of straight near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments are observed in both images; the right micrograph clearly shows that some dipole segments are dragged out of glissile screw dislocations when the dislocations encounter obstacles. These superdislocations are B2 lattice superdislocations composed of two 1/2<111> partial dislocations with small spacing due to high antiphase boundary fault energy. This figure provides direct microstructural evidence for the work hardening mechanism of high-Al Fe-Al alloys, confirming that dislocation dipole formation occurs during deformation.

This figure presents a pair of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs acquired under +g and -g imaging conditions, for a deformed Fe-35at.%Al alloy sample with B2 ordered structure, which was compressed to 2-3% plastic strain at room temperature. The scale bar is 0.5μm, the zone axis is near [1 1 3], and the applied imaging vector is of 1 1 0 type. Numerous edge dipole segments of superdislocations (marked as d and indicated by arrows) are clearly visible in the two micrographs: these superdislocations are B2 lattice superdislocations composed of two (1/2)<111> partial dislocations, which stay close to each other due to high anti-phase boundary energy. The dipole segments are generated when moving screw superdislocations encounter obstacles during deformation. This figure confirms the high density of dislocation dipoles in deformed Fe-35%Al, supporting the conclusion that dislocation dipole formation contributes to the work hardening behavior of Fe-Al intermetallic alloys.

This is a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph of room-temperature deformed Fe-35at.%Al alloy, with a scale bar of 0.25 μm. The observation is conducted under a zone axis near [1 1 3], with the 1 1 0 imaging vector marked by the arrow. The micrograph shows discrete dislocation segments and weak background fringes. The observed dislocations are B2 lattice superdislocations, consisting of two (1/2)<1 1 1> partial dislocations spaced closely due to high anti-phase boundary (APB) energy in this alloy. The weak background fringes correspond to remnant APB defects from superdislocation motion destroying minor DO3 order in the alloy. This micrograph provides direct microstructural evidence for dislocation characteristics in high-Al ordered Fe-Al alloys, supporting work hardening mechanism analysis for ordered Fe-Al intermetallics, and confirms no four-partial superdislocations exist in Fe-35%Al.
